title: Belief Disagreement and Portfolio Choice
abstract: Using proprietary financial data on millions of households, we show that likely-Republicans increased the equity share and market beta of their portfolios following the 2016 presidential election, while likely-Democrats rebalanced into safe assets. We provide evidence that this behavior was driven by investors interpreting public information based on different models of the world. We use detailed controls to rule out the main nonbelief-based channels such as income hedging needs, preferences, and local economic exposures. These findings are driven by a small share of investors making big changes, and are stronger among investors who trade more ex ante.

article.introduction:
The canonical assumption in mosteconomic models is that people have rational expectations with common priors. However, in both the laboratory and the real world, people tend to report different probabilities for the same event, and report updating these beliefs differently in response to common information.1A leading interpretation of this evidence is that people believe in different models of the world—they hold different and possibly dogmatic priors—which leads them to disagree about baseline probabilities and about the implications of common information for future outcomes. However, a major challenge in testing this proposition outside of artificial laboratory environments is distinguishing behavioral responses that come from changes in beliefs separately from behavioral responses that come through other economic channels or that are driven by other dimensions of preferences.2In this paper, we test this proposition not by inferring beliefs from reported beliefs or asset prices, but by showing that investors adjust their portfolios differently in response to the same public event and by ruling out the main nonbelief channels through which the event may have caused this behavior.
Specifically, we posit that a person's political affiliation captures the model of the world that they use to interpret the economic impact of political outcomes. We then study portfolio differences by political affiliation (inferred from zip code) using anonymized, proprietary data on millions ofRetirement Investors(RIs), defined as retail investors with retirement savings accounts in the middle 80% of the age-adjusted distribution of retirement wealth.3Consistent with surveys in which Republicans (Democrats) report becoming more optimistic (pessimistic) about future U.S. economic performance following the U.S. national election of 2016, we show that likely-Republicans rebalanced their portfolios toward equity, while likely-Democrats rebalanced their portfolios toward relatively safe assets after the unexpected election outcome, both over a six- to nine-month horizon following the election.
The key challenge for identifying the role of beliefs in this portfolio rebalancing is that, even if investors held common priors and updated rationally, the election could still differentially change their real hedging needs or overall wealth levels. For example, Republican and Democratic voters tend to work in different industries and live in different parts of the country, and thus, their future incomes and costs of living might be differentially affected by the election outcome.4Such economic differences could lead to behavioral differences not driven by differences in beliefs. Previous research (discussed below) documents both different economic impacts of election outcomes by political party and a relationship between reported beliefs and behavior. Our key contribution beyond more accurate measurement is that we rule out channels through which the election may have differentially impacted individuals' portfolio behavior other than beliefs.
Our main finding is that likely-Republicans increased the equity share and market beta of their portfolios relative tootherwise similarlikely-Democrats conditional on detailed controls both for ex ante differences in preferences and endowments and for ex post differences in local economic conditions and income hedging needs. That is, differences in portfolio rebalancing between Republican and Democratic voters following the election are not explained by differences in ex ante wealth, income, age, or characteristics of portfolios. More importantly, rebalancing is not explained by differential exposure to regional or firm-level policies due to the election outcome: we continue to find differences in investment behavior when comparing investors working at the same employer and living in the same county, that is, after controlling for employer-county-period fixed effects. Further, the documented changes in portfolio holdings are due to active rebalancing net of any differences in passive portfolio appreciation. Finally, the outcome of the 2016 election was an unlikely event, the policy differences between presidential candidates were large, and the changes in the portfolio share of equity were similar for Republicans and Democrats in the year prior to the election, which together suggest that the differential rebalancing was a response to the election rather than other differences correlated with party affiliation.
Having controlled for the main channels through which rational theories operate, we conclude that the differential rebalancing behavior is due to differential updating of beliefs about the expected return of stock market investments, likely through the performance of the U.S. economy. Consistent with this interpretation, people affiliated with either party reported little change in their views about their own individual economic situations but reported significantly different changes by party about their expectations of macrolevel economic prospects, consistent with Huberman et al. (2018). However, we cannot reject the possibility thatarbitrarypreference variation by party affiliation drove differences in both reported beliefs and investment behavior.5
Quantitatively, the average effect of the election on portfolio allocations grows slowly over the months following the election, suggesting that investors understand that they disagree with other market participants rather than that they believe that they have a temporary informational advantage. After a year, investors in the quintile of most-Republican zip codes increased the equity share of their portfolios by roughly three-quarters of 1% relative to investors in the quintile of most-Democratic zip codes. This relatively small average result masks substantial investor-level heterogeneity. Only a third of investors actively changed their portfolio in any given year and only a 10thof investors changed their allocations across asset classes (consistent with the passivity documented in Madrian and Shea (2001) and Choi et al. (2004)). Among the investors who did rebalance, the election had a much larger effect: 75% of our effect comes from investors who changed the equity share of their portfolio by more than 25% after the election. Further, among those investors who actively reallocated their portfolios during the year before our main sample starts, the effect of the election on equity share is three times as large.
This heterogeneity in responses also varies by how actively investors manage their portfolios. Wealthier and older investors, who tend to be more attentive to their savings, rebalanced more strongly in response to the election, while investors who delegate more, who have a larger fraction of their wealth invested in automatic investment products such as target date funds or accounts managed by an advisor, rebalanced less in response to the election.
Survey-reported beliefs are consistent with our conclusion that differential updating of beliefs drives portfolio behavior. We follow Curtin (2017) and show that Republicans in the University of Michigan Survey of Consumers (UMSC) reported becoming much more optimistic about the future of the U.S. economy following the 2016 U.S. national election, while Democrats reported becoming more pessimistic.6This pattern of reported beliefs is consistent with a substantial literature documenting that political affiliations lead people to report that they interpret public news differently.7
More importantly, our results also provide evidence that survey-reported beliefs on the macroeconomy contain substantial information about beliefs that are independent of people's individual economic situations or preferences. Specifically, by identifying an event that changes beliefs and controlling for the main channels through which the event could have changed portfolios other than through beliefs, we show that heterogeneous beliefs drive both reported beliefs and differential portfolio allocations. While survey beliefs are qualitative, the combination of a large swing in beliefs and a small change in behavior is in line with the attenuation puzzle, that is, the low correlation between portfolio holdings and reported beliefs across investors, documented by Ameriks et al. (2020), and the finding that changes in reported beliefs do not predict the likelihood of trading but do predict the direction of trading conditional on a trade, documented by Giglio et al. (2021).
Our results support models in which people agree to disagree even in asset markets in which prices convey information. As noted, the rebalancing that we find occurs over many months following the election, consistent with investors being aware that asset prices do not rapidly incorporate their party-specific beliefs and hence that they need not react immediately to such changes in beliefs. As additional evidence supporting the predictions of theoretical models with heterogeneous (non-Bayesian) updating of beliefs (Miller (1977), Harrison and Kreps (1978), Harris and Raviv (1993), Morris (1996), Scheinkman and Xiong (2003), Banerjee and Kremer (2010), Simsek (2013)), we show that the total amount of trading among the investors in our sample rises following the election. Rational expectations models of asset prices have been largely unable to match portfolio heterogeneity and the volume of trading.8Finally, in a back-of-the-envelope calculation, we show that the changes in beliefs and behavior induced by the 2016 election only had a small quantitative effect on the net demand for stocks, on the order of a few billion dollars.
While there are many existing approaches to modeling differences in beliefs, our results most directly support models that link heterogeneity in beliefs to identity.9In particular, building on the model of Akerlof and Kranton (2000), Bénabou and Tirole (2011) contains a model of stake-dependent beliefs in which people's investment choices are affected by what their allocations imply about their political beliefs. A political identity comes with the belief that the set of policies associated with that identity are good policies. In this context, identity theory predicts that people's portfolios reflect their set of beliefs, so that Democrats (Republicans) trade on the belief that the change in policies following the election will be bad (good) for the national economy and the stock market.
By controlling for county of residence, we control for differences in the supply of information such as differences in local newspapers or access to cable news. Our findings are therefore consistent with differences in beliefs being “deep,” like identity, and driving differences in information sources rather than the reverse.
While a growing literature studies the effect of political polarization in the United States on both economic outcomes and reported beliefs, prior papers find inconclusive or no effects of elections on household behavior. Most closely related to our paper, Bonaparte, Kumar, and Page (2017) find no significant or stable correlation during the period 1988 to 2000 between investors' party affiliations and the equity shares of their portfolios using self-reported data from the National Longitudinal Survey of Youth.10Kaustia and Torstila (2011) show that left-wing voters in Finland are less likely to hold stocks than right-wing voters, but also do not control for differences in the exposure of labor income or local economic performance to political risks. We advance this line of research both by studying a historically large political shock and by using much larger and more detailed administrative data on millions of typical investors and controlling for the main direct channels through which changes in policies could differentially impact investors. Our work is also related to papers that document differences in professional behaviors by political affiliation (Hong and Kostovetsky (2012), Kempf and Tsoutsoura (2021)). Consistent with our findings but using an individual's professional situation to capture different models of the world, Linnainmaa, Melzer, and Previtero (2021) show that professional portfolio advice correlates with personal portfolio choices across investment advisers (see also Cheng, Raina, and Xiong (2014)). Again, our contribution is to rule out channels through which rational theories unrelated to differences in beliefs could rationalize observed differences in behavior.
Our results are also related to research showing that investors seem to incorrectly update in response to idiosyncratic or private information, typically by overweighting information from their own past experiences or friends (Grinblatt and Keloharju (2000), Vissing-Jørgensen (2003), Malmendier and Nagel (2011), Cookson and Niessner (2020), Bailey et al. (2018)).
Some research also studies whether local aggregate consumer spending responds differently to electoral outcomes depending on the dominant local political affiliation (e.g., Gerber and Huber (2009), Gerber and Huber (2010), Mian, Sufi, and Khoshkhou (2021)). These analyses focus on measuring local aggregate effects, and hence, do not seek to separate the beliefs channel from differences in real economic effects. We contribute to this literature in SectionIX.Aby analyzing the role of beliefs in differences in saving rates.
Finally, our paper also contributes to an older literature that provides indirect evidence that investors interpret public signals differently. The volume of asset trading rises after public signals (e.g., Kandel and Pearson (1995)), survey beliefs or stated forecasts widen (e.g., Bamber, Barron, and Stober (1999), Carlin, Longstaff, and Matoba (2014)), and stock prices move in predictable ways (see the survey Hong and Stein (2007)) that are correlated with economic disagreement (Li and Li (2021)).
The remainder of this paper is structured as follows. In SectionI, we discuss the public event that we study. SectionIIpresents survey evidence on changes in beliefs around the event. SectionIIIdescribes the data that we use to analyze household portfolios. SectionIVpresents our main findings on portfolio reallocation. In SectionV, we perform quantitative analysis in which we control for alternative, nonbelief-based channels. SectionVIdiscusses the role of portfolio inaction. SectionVIIexamines variation in trading volume. In SectionVIII, we study heterogeneity in portfolio rebalancing. SectionIXpresents additional results. Finally, SectionXconcludes.
article.I. The 2016 U.S. Election:
The 2016 U.S. national election is almost uniquely suited for measuring how households who believe in different political models of the world update in response to a publicly observable signal, for four reasons. First, the outcome of the 2016 national election was a public event,11and thus, our results are unlikely to be due to differences in attention. Conditional on observing the outcome, households may process the information at different speeds or take different amounts of time to act, but there is no ex ante reason to believe that this would differ by party affiliation.
Second, the 2016 election was not correlated with other significant events. Such a correlation would weaken our claim that the differences we document are due to different interpretations of news about future economic policies and performance.
Third, the outcome of the 2016 presidential election, and the Republican party achieving a majority in the U.S. Senate, constitute a large and unexpected change in future U.S. economic policy. While all presidential elections affect policy, the 2016 election involved two candidates with quite different policy prescriptions. More importantly, the outcome of the election was unexpected. For most other types of news about the economy or other changes in governance, such as most elections, legislation, and court decisions, information percolates into the economy more slowly and the timing of its arrival is hard to pinpoint.
For a measure of the probability of the presidential election outcome, we consider the market prices of two contracts traded on Betfair that pay $1 conditional on the respective party winning the election. State prices of course reflect marginal utilities as well as probabilities, but Betfair is a U.K.-based internet betting exchange and U.S. regulations make it hard for American investors to enter this market.12The prices of the two Betfair contracts of interest therefore differ from probabilities only to the extent that the marginal utility of U.K. investors is different across outcomes of the U.S. presidential election. The market predicted a Democratic victory the entire year before the election, with a roughly 75% likelihood during the six months prior to the election and an over 80% likelihood on the eve of the election (seeInternet AppendixFigureIA.1).13This stability contrasts slightly with polling data (which are survey-based hypothetical choice data), which shows larger swings around events like national conventions and in which the odds appeared close to even at times.
Fourth, we can use differences in political affiliation as ex ante measures of differences in investors' models of the world. This measure is not derived from economic behavior like portfolio allocation, for which differences might be due to differences in preferences such as risk aversion or differences in income dynamics and resulting hedging needs. That said, a correlation between political affiliation and preference or hedging needs is, of course, possible, since beliefs about the economy are not randomly assigned, but rather form endogenously. Thus, while there are ex ante differences in the portfolios of Republicans and Democrats on average, a focus of our analysis is to provide evidence that changes in portfolio allocations in response to the election are not driven by these ex ante differences in economic exposure.14A related benefit is that we can analyze other household-level data that contain measures of political affiliation to show how household responses differ along other dimensions; in particular, we present analysis of survey measures of economic beliefs in SectionII.
All four features of this event are important for our study and for any study of the relationship between beliefs and behavior. In our case, people of different party affiliations are continually responding to various pieces of information and changes in their economic environment. Our hypothesis is that they may be responding differently along many dimensions of identity. Without the ability to differentiate households ex ante and without a large, public signal, we would not be able to separate households' belief-driven portfolio responses to election outcomes from responses to other factors.15As a result, we would not expect to find the same (absolute value of the) difference in rebalancing by party that we document in response to the 2016 election for every U.S. presidential election. In SectionIX.D, we discuss this point further in relation to analysis of differences in portfolio behavior by party around the 2012 election.
article.II. Reported Beliefs:
Prior research shows that Republicans and Democrats interpret political events differently and that this leads to different reported economic expectations.16In this section, we build directly on Curtin (2016,2017), showing that in the UMSC, Republicans report much more optimism about the economy following the election, while Democrats report more pessimism.17We use both monthly data on reported economic expectations typically in the UMSC and special questions that the survey added about political affiliation from June to October 2016 and from February to April 2017. The survey does not contain zip code or county of residence, and we cannot replicate our sample of RIs in the UMSC, so instead, we analyze the subsample of households that hold stocks (63% of the weighted sample, 65% of the unweighted sample). The results are nearly identical for the entire UMSC sample.
We first find that the election appears to have had a dramatic effect on expectations of future national economic performance. Figure1, Panel A, shows the average response among people with different party affiliations to the question “Looking ahead, which would you say is more likely – that in the country as a whole we'll have continuous good times during the next 5 years or so, or that we will have periods of widespread unemployment or depression, or what?” We index the answers such that Good times = 5, Good with qualifications = 4, Pro-con = 3, Bad with qualifications = 2, and Bad times = 1. On average, Republican expectations changed from slightly pessimistic before the election to highly optimistic after, while expectations of Democrats show the reverse pattern. Confirming this difference, Figure1, Panel B, shows the same large changes in expectations of the unemployment rate in one year. In theory, if these large changes in reported beliefs about future national economic performance reflect changes in decision-theoretic beliefs, then they should lead to a desire to change exposures to national economic performance in investment portfolios (at a given price of the stock market).
Our second result is that there were no similar large changes in people's reported expectations about their own economic circumstances. Figure2, Panel A, plots by party affiliation the average answer to the question “During the next 12 months, do you expect your income to be higher or lower than during the past year?” Before the election, Democrats were more likely to expect higher income, but the advantage is small. Following the election, Republicans on average reported higher expected income and Democrats lower, but the changes are moderate and the ultimate differences again small. In contrast, Figure2, Panel B, which depicts changes in expectations regarding whether business conditions overall will be better or worse in a year, shows a much larger swing. This result suggests that changes in people's behavior after the election were due to changes in beliefs about the aggregate economy and not to changes in beliefs about their own individual economic situations, such as changes in expected labor income that might make them more or less likely to invest in equity.
article.III. Data:
The particular measure of different models of the world that we use is political party affiliation. Our main measure of likely political affiliation is based on publicly available data on individual campaign donations during the 2015 to 2016 election cycle from the Federal Election Commission aggregated to the zip code level. We restrict attention to contributions from individuals to political action committees associated with the two main parties or with their presidential nominees and with at least $20 million in donations (see SectionIof theInternet Appendixfor further details). For each zip code, we count the number of donors to each party.18We limit our analysis to zip codes with at least 10 donating individuals and define theRepublican contribution shareof a zip code as the number of donors to the Republican party or the Republican presidential nominee divided by the total number of donors in that zip code.Internet AppendixFigureIA.2shows the geographical distribution of the Republican contribution share.
For robustness, we confirm that our results hold for three alternative measures of likely political affiliation. First, we consider the dollar-weighted version of Republican contribution share, defined as the dollars donated to the Republication party or the Republican presidential nominee divided by the total amount donated in the zip code to either party. Second, we use data on votes for the 2016 presidential election at the county level and define the Republican share of a county as the number of votes for the Republican candidate divided by the total number of votes to both parties' candidates.19FigureIA.3shows that the Republican contribution share and Republican vote share line up well at the county level, despite the limited number of individuals who make individual contributions (the median coverage by zip code is 0.3% of the population). Finally, we use county-level vote shares for the Republican presidential candidate in the 2012 election (eliminating any sui generis effects of particular candidates in 2016). All of our measures surely suffer from some mismeasurement. To the extent that mismeasurement is classical, our main results are attenuated.
Our main data are anonymized account-level data on financial holdings from a large U.S. financial institution. We have access to anonymized information on all accounts held directly at the firm by individual investors. For these accounts, we observe end-of-month account balances and holdings, and all inflows, outflows, and transfers at a daily frequency. We observe assets at the security level (identified by CUSIP) for 92% of wealth. For the remaining 8%, we observe the characteristics of the fund the wealth is invested in. We aggregate all accounts—pretax, taxable, and untaxed—across all members of a household and track household portfolios.20The data cover millions of households and trillions of dollars in financial wealth.
We also have access to some information on the investors themselves. In addition to wealth information on all households, we observe age, gender, marital status, and zip code for the vast majority of individuals.21We define the head of the household by selecting the individual with the highest total assets (or the older individual if all assets are jointly held), and we use the head's characteristics as those of the household for nonfinancial information like age and for employment information. We can construct an employer indicator variable for a large subset of households. In addition, using the employer's NAICS code, we assign each such household to a three-digit industry. For a subset of these households, we also observe annual labor income of the head of household. Our main sample uses information from one year before to one year after the election, from October 31, 2015 to October 31, 2017.
While these data provide a unique view of retirement savings and the portfolio allocations of U.S. households, there are two potential weaknesses of our data. First, while we observe a significant share of U.S. households, the sample is obviously not a randomly selected sample. In particular, most observed household wealth is retirement savings and few households have a very high net worth (as we document below). Our analysis does not require a random sample, but we would like to understand the relationship between our sample and the U.S. population. The second potential weakness is that we do not necessarily observe all of the investable wealth of the households in our sample, as we only observe one member of the household for some households, and some households have investable wealth at other institutions.
To address these issues, we select a subsample of our data that meets a set of criteria and thus is likely to be reasonably representative of the U.S. population that also meets this set of criteria. We focus on households with moderate levels of retirement wealth, which we call RIs. Specifically, we define RIs as households of working age—heads of households between the ages of 25 and 64—without extremely high or low retirement wealth, defined as all wealth in retirement saving accounts of all types (excluding defined benefit plans and Social Security).
We use the 2016 Survey of Consumer Finances (SCF) to show how the population of RIs compares to the U.S. population and how well the wealth and portfolio holdings of the RI subsample of our account-level data compare to the RI subsample in the United States. We focus on households with some retirement wealth. Using the 2016 SCF, we run quantile regressions of the log of retirement wealth on a second-order polynomial in age. We then drop households with retirement wealth below the estimated 10thpercentile or above the 90thby age. This RI subsample of the SCF captures 31.4% of the U.S. population, 32.0% of retirement wealth, and 24.8% of investable wealth held by households in the United States according to the SCF,22where investable wealth is defined as money market funds (MMFs), non-MMFs, individual stocks and bonds, quasi-liquid retirement wealth, and other managed accounts.23Figure3shows the wealth distributions of both the U.S. population and our subsample in the 2016 SCF.
Applying the same cutoffs to our data provides a sample of millions of investors and well more than a trillion dollars in investable wealth.24Panel A of Figure4shows that the retirement wealth distribution of our RI sample of households lines up well with that measured by the SCF. Panel B of Figure4shows that our data also match reasonably well the distribution of total investable wealth in the SCF, but our data are missing some nonretirement wealth mainly for households with more than $500,000 in investable wealth.
To characterize portfolio risk-taking, we classify fund and security holdings into equity, bonds, cash, and alternative assets (e.g., real estate and precious metals).25We calculate market betas by regressing fund and security excess returns on the market excess return over the period 2006 to 2019, requiring at least 24 months of return observations. We also use other security-level information, such as international and sector exposures. SectionIIof theInternet Appendixprovides more details.
Column (1) of TableIsummarizes the portfolios held by our RI sample, as of just prior to the election shock. Our average RI has $138,100 in investable wealth, of which 84% is in retirement accounts. More than 70% of wealth is invested in equity (the sum of directly held equity, equity funds, and the equity amount of funds that invest across asset types), and the portfolios have an average market beta of 0.74 (0.72 if households are weighted by wealth). Column (2) shows that, relative to the estimates from the SCF, our data capture most retirement wealth of RIs. Column (2) also shows that while we match median total investable wealth well, our data miss the mean of nonretirement investable wealth, primarily due to missing wealth among high-net-worth households (see Figure4).
Column (1) of TableIIshows that the average age of household heads in our RI sample is 46, 44% of household heads are female, and 73% of heads are married. Our sample of working age households has a similar age distribution as the corresponding SCF sample of RIs (see FigureIA.4). The average household income is $101,200 and the median is $77,300.
Figure5, Panel A, shows a scrambled map of the share of households in each U.S. county that are in our sample of RIs. We calculate the population share of households who are in our sample by county, and then randomly reallocate the shares across counties in each state to preserve anonymity. Figure5, Panel B, shows the density of the share of the population in each county that is in our RI data set. We remind the reader that RI households represent only a third of the U.S. population (according to the SCF) and that we are using the term “household” when we may only observe one of two (or more) earning and investing members.
Finally, we use each investor's zip code of residence one year prior to the election to link investors to our zip code or county measures of political party affiliation. Our sample is tilted toward households who live in Democratic zip codes according to our contributions measure.26
TablesIandIIshow the distribution of asset holdings and demographics by party affiliation of households' zip code just prior to the election. We observe differences across zip codes associated with party affiliation, but they are generally small. The average equity share varies by less than 1% across groups, with Democratic zip codes having slightly higher equity shares on average. The differences are even smaller for the average market beta of investor portfolios. Nevertheless, in our analysis, we control for preelection equity share, as well as many individual demographic characteristics.
While both the share of equity and the market beta of the portfolios of households with different political affiliations are quite similar prior to the election, we do find some differences in the composition of equity holdings. While equity portfolios are similar across zip codes in terms of market betas, the share allocated to funds, and the share of international equity holdings, there are differences in the sectoral composition of stock holdings and in particular with respect to industry performance immediately following the election.27Although allocations to specific sectors are a limited fraction of overall portfolios, we find that households in zip codes with different predominant political affiliations hold significantly different exposures to winning and losing sectors. TableIshows that the most Republican zip codes hold on average 6.5% of their total equity in winning sectors, relative to 4.8% for the most Democratic zip codes. For losing industries, the picture is reversed with the most Democratic zip codes holding more than 4.7% of their equity in losing industries and the most Republican zip codes holding less than 3.5%.
Given these differences, it is important that our analysis in the next two sections shows that these ex ante sectoral differences in portfolio holdings do not explain our findings of differential postelection movements in equity shares, trading, and portfolio betas by party affiliation. We do this in three ways. First, we control for the differences in ex ante portfolio composition in regression analysis. Second, we analyze price-constant portfolios that exclude all postelection differences in portfolios returns. Third, this difference in holdings is due largely to people overweighting their employer's stock in their portfolios, and hence, much of our analysis compares workers at the same firm.
article.IV. Household Portfolio Reallocation:
This section shows that likely-Republican households increased the exposure of their portfolios to U.S. economic growth after the 2016 presidential election relative to likely-Democratic households. We show that this effect was not driven by passive appreciation but instead by trading of households who actively reallocated their portfolios. The next section builds the case that this effect is due to differential updating of beliefs by showing that this finding is robust to many controls for differences in preferences or hedging needs across households.
To begin, we simply plot the data. Figure6shows the change in the average portfolio share of equities (relative to October 31, 2016) for households in zip codes with different shares of contributions to each party, relative to their baseline shares at the end of October 2016. Panel A displays equally weighted portfolio shares, and Panel B displays the value-weighted portfolio shares, which sum the value of equity across all households in each group before dividing by total investable wealth for that group.
First, for all groups, the share of wealth invested in equities decreases prior to the election, which suggests that something caused reduced investment in the stock market regardless of party affiliation.28This rebalancing might have been election-related—due, for example, to information revealed during the political race or to the uncertainty of the upcoming election—or it could simply represent rebalancing after half a decade of relatively good stock market returns. After the election, equity shares rebound for all groups, due primarily to high stock market returns.
Second—and our main result—the share of wealth invested in equities rose in predominantly Republican zip codes relative to predominantly Democratic zip codes following the election. Specifically, households living in Republican zip codes increased their equity exposure by roughly 0.4% of their wealth relative to those in the most-Democratic zip codes, consistent with increased optimism about the economy by Republicans relative to Democrats.
Third, there were minimal differences in the evolution of the portfolio shares in equity across areas with different political affiliations prior to the election. During this time, information was arriving about the likely outcome of the election, but the total change in probability over this period prior to the election was smaller than that on the one day of the election (see FigureIA.1). The similarity of the movements in the equity share before the election is consistent with our evidence of only small prior differences in equity shares across zip codes with different political leanings. These zip codes are surely not the same, but they are also not affected very differently by economic news (and the limited political news) in the year prior to the election.29
We find similar results for long bonds and cash-like securities. Households in zip codes that are predominantly Republican decrease the shares of their portfolios invested in bonds and cash-like assets (including MMMFs) following the election relative to those in zip codes that are predominantly Democratic (see FiguresIA.6andIA.7).
One concern with the results so far is the possibility that these movements may be due to inactivity, initial differences in holdings, and appreciation resulting from the postelection stock market increase following the election. Republicans may hold more equity or equities with higher beta, and thus, might have had a disproportionate increase in the equity share of their portfolios due to the postelection increase in stock prices. Like most wealth for the typical American household, the majority of the wealth that we observe is retirement wealth, which is notably “sleepy.” While we address these concerns in a regression analysis below, here we present three pieces of evidence that reject this hypothesis visually.
First, we find larger differential effects by party affiliation when we focus on households who are more active in the past, where an “active” household is defined as one that had at least one monthly portfolio reallocation due to trading that changed the equity share by at least 5% (in absolute value) during the year November 2014 to October 2015 (the year prior to our main sample). This sample is 11.1% of our original sample (see TableII). Figure7, Panel A, shows that active investors in mostly Republican zip codes had a much larger relative increase in equity share after the election, on the order of 1.5% of their investable wealth on average. (Panels A and B in FigureIA.8show the corresponding plots for long bonds and safe assets, respectively, for the sample of active investors.) These effects translate into differences in the overall market betas of portfolios by likely political affiliation. Figure7, Panel B, shows that movements in market betas of active investors are also very similar preelection across political affiliations, and diverge postelection, with market beta rising relatively more in Republican zip codes postelection.
Second, we measure the difference in active rebalancing in two ways. The composition of an investor's portfolio is changed by revaluations, reallocations, and inflows and outflows related to deposits and withdrawals. We measure rebalancing into equity by focusing on changes in equity share driven by either transfers of assets into or out of equity or withdrawals or inflows that change equity shares.
Our first method to measure rebalancing is simply to construct and track hypothetical portfolios as if there were no changes in valuations of funds or securities. Figure8, Panel A, plots the equity share of these hypothetical price-constant portfolios and shows that prior to the election, households were rebalancing out of equity in a nearly identical way across the distribution of zip codes by political affiliation. After the election, Republican zip codes actively increased their equity shares by reallocating wealth from safe assets into equity, while primarily Democratic zip codes decreased their equity shares by reallocating wealth out of equity into bonds and cash. The total reallocation is roughly the same size as the total difference shown in Figure6. In Figure8, Panel A, we also observe that mostly Republican zip codes reduce their equity shares slightly more than other zip codes over the year prior to the election, which is consistent with the small amount of news that comes out preelection as the chances of a Democratic victory rise. Figure8, Panel B, again shows similar patterns for market betas before and after the election. (Panels A and B in FigureIA.9show the corresponding plots for long bonds and safe assets, respectively, of price-constant portfolios.)
It is worth noting that while Figure6shows that party affiliation and the election do not explain much of the temporal variation in zip code between the most-Republican and the most-Democratic zip codes, most of this is due to passive behavior and market returns. Removing market returns, Figure8, Panel A, shows that a much higher share of the temporal variation in equity share between these groups is driven by political variation in beliefs.
While these results present the data in a transparent manner, they do not show that changes in beliefs were the driving force for these reallocations rather than differences in preferences or differential changes in hedging needs due to differential changes in the stochastic process of labor income or the local economy. Much existing research relates reported beliefs to economic behaviors (see Manski (2018)), and to portfolio choice in particular (e.g., Vissing-Jørgensen (2003), Ameriks et al. (2020), Giglio et al. (2021)). In general, however, many factors change both behavior and beliefs, and thus, may contaminate any analysis of the effect of beliefs on behavior. Because we have identified a particular source of variation in beliefs, we can address the main ways in which the election may have directly impacted behavior other than through beliefs. For example, the election outcome raised the probability of reductions in personal and corporate tax rates, changes in personal tax deductions through itemization, increases in barriers to trade, and reductions in various regulations, and the stock prices of companies in different industries were impacted quite differently. To the extent that affiliates of one party or the other are impacted differently by these policies, or tend to work for or live near winning industries, the political outcome may differentially affect their future incomes and cost of living. The next section provides evidence that the differences in rebalancing are not driven by the different economic effects of the election but rather by different interpretations of the election. These results thus lend credence to the informativeness of reported expectations data.
article.V. Quantitative Analysis Controlling for:
We emphasize that the effects of our control variables are allowed to differ over time, just like the effect of the Republican share. Thus, when we control for a variable, we are not controlling for its average effect preelection and postelection, but rather we are allowing for a different effect of this control variable in every period.
We start our quantitative analysis by running regressions in quarterly first differences. We use portfolios exactly one year before the election to construct initial positions and create a sample of portfolio holdings every three months, starting with the end of January 2016 and ending with the end of October 2017, so we have four observations of portfolio changes before the election (denotedt=−3,−2,−1$t=-3,-2,-1$, and 0 for October 31, 2016) and four observations after the election (t=1,2,3,4$t=1,2,3,4$) for each individual.
To show that the differences in portfolio rebalancing that we observe are due to differences in beliefs rather than differential economic outcomes or hedging demands, we make use of many control variables,Xi,t−1$X_{i,t-1}$, related to differences in preferences, incomes, other wealth, or the ways these factors are exposed to economic policies. Our baseline control variables include the lagged portfolio equity share, age, gender, marital status, log lagged financial wealth, and log labor income in 2015, which are, as noted, all interacted with quarterly indicator variables. These controls are designed to confirm that our quantitative results are not biased by initial differences in wealth and portfolios that might be due to heterogeneity in, for example, risk aversion, as investors who differ ex ante in endowments or risk preferences select different portfolios and are therefore differentially affected by the election outcome. Portfolio allocations are also significantly mean-reverting, which is most obvious at the extremes of the distribution of initial portfolio shares, since shares are bounded by zero and one. However, mean-rev occurs across the whole distribution. We additionally control for ex ante differences in sectoral allocations (as discussed at the end of SectionIII) and for zip code house price growth between 2010 and 2015 in the baseline. We use the all-homes Zillow Home Value Index (ZHVI) as a measure of house prices at the zip code level.
Panel A of Figure9shows the cumulative change, relative to the end of October 2016, in the equity share of households in Republican zip codes relative to those in Democratic zip codes based on this baseline regression.30During the first three months following the election, people in a zip code with only Republican donations increased their equity holdings by 0.40% of their portfolio relative to a zip code with only Democratic donations. This share rose to 0.65% by the end of the second three-month period following the election. The cumulative effect further increased slightly between 6 and 12 months after the election.
Since equity shares also reflect changes in asset valuations, and not only active decisions by account owners, we rerun the baseline regression using the hypothetical price-constant equity share measure from SectionIVas the outcome variable. This measure is driven purely by active rebalancing decisions. Panel B of Figure9shows similar effects for this measure. We use the transactions-based price-constant equity share measure as our main outcome variable in later tests.
Finally, we control for the possible channels through which the election outcome and the dynamics of future policy might impact investors differentially through their employer or locality, in which case their portfolio behavior may change for reasons other than changes in beliefs. To do so, we include a fixed effect for each employer-county pair at each point in time. This version of equation (2) then compares, in each quarter, investors working at the same firm and living in the same county while still controlling for the effect of all time-invariant, individual-level characteristics. Panel C of Figure9shows the effects of the election outcome on investors' equity shares controlling for these employer-county-period fixed effects. These quarterly regressions show the same impact of the election on portfolios: Democrats reduced their holdings of equity, while Republicans increased theirs. Panel D of Figure9shows that these results are due to differences in active trading and not to differences in passive appreciation.31
TableIIIreports estimates of equation (3) for changes in the equity share of price-constant portfolios (the corresponding findings for realized equity shares are inInternet AppendixTableIA.IV). For consistency, we restrict the regression sample to RIs for which we observe a complete set of controls, so that sample sizes do not change across columns. This subsample of households with complete controls comprises 27.7% of the full RI sample. In SectionIII.Bof theInternet Appendix, we show that our findings are not driven by this sample selection and that the results extend to less restrictive samples.
The first column of TableIIIreports estimates for the specification without any controls. As before, we find that likely-Republican households significantly increased their equity holdings relative to likely-Democratic households after the election shock. In the remaining columns, we show that the main effect is similar after controlling for a large number of variables that measure possible differences in wealth or hedging needs that might be correlated with the political affiliation of zip code of residence.
The second column reports results when we include the baseline set of controls. In the year prior to the election, likely-Republicans slightly reduced their equity share relative to likely-Democrats, whereas after the election, likely-Republicans significantly increased their equity share relative to likely-Democrats. The total postelection difference is 0.8, matching the cumulative effect from the quarterly panel regression with individual fixed effects as reported in Panel B of FigureIA.17.
To control for ex post differences in wealth growth, column (3) includes ex post realized income growth from 2016 to 2017 and house price growth between 2015 and 2017. Columns (4) and (5) control for remaining differences in hedging needs due to employment opportunities. Specifically, column (4) includes an indicator variable for employer industry (three-digit NAICS),32while column (5) takes this control a step further by including indicators for each employer year. By controlling for employer effects, we absorb any differences across households that are due to Republicans and Democrats working for different firms and hence being differentially affected by the election outcome in their expected labor income or due to differences in retirement investment menu.
In columns (6) and (7), we continue to find that Republicans increased their relative equity share when we add state and county indicators to the controls. These indicators capture differences in local economic conditions that may be related to urbanicity, manufacturing shares, and firms' exposure to import competition (Feenstra (1996), Barrot, Loualiche, and Sauvagnat (2019)). These indicators also control for the local effects of economic policies that might lead to ex post heterogeneity in how the election outcome impacts wealth and hedging needs by location. It is worth reemphasizing that, as in the previous columns, all controls are interacted with annual indicators. We find that the locational controls decrease the magnitude of our main finding only slightly.
Ultimately in column (8), we control for the main ways in which rational hedging demand might generate differential changes in response to the election due to differences in how employers or local economies are impacted by the election. Specifically, we control for employer-county-period fixed effects, which implies that our estimates are based on comparing people working for the same employer and living in the same county but living in zip codes with different party affiliations in a given year. We find that the magnitudes on our main effects are highly significant although somewhat reduced. However, these indicator variables may be absorbing some valid variation, since our main measure of the effect of beliefs is a noisy proxy for party affiliation at the zip code level.
The results in column (8) of TableIIIhave interesting implications for the interpretation of our findings related to work on differences in media and political beliefs, as discussed in the introduction. By controlling for county, we are controlling largely for access to information since people in the same county likely have access to the same media, for example. Thus, to the extent that variation in beliefs across zip codes within a county is related to differences in the consumption of media, these differences represent differences in the demand for certain types of information. That is, we are not measuring the effect of exogenous differences in media consumption, but rather the expression of individual (or rather zip-code-level) beliefs.33
Our main results are quite similar when using alternative measures of likely political affiliation in 2016 and when using the 2012 Republican vote share by county (see TableIA.V). Thus, our results are not driven by something particular to donations (as opposed to votes) or to the candidates in 2016 (although we do not have a way to adjust for differences in turnout). At the one-year horizon with employer-state-period fixed effects, we find no statistically significant evidence that changes over time in the intensity of county support matter above and beyond the 2012 vote shares.
In sum, the regression analysis reported in this section confirms that the public signal of the 2016 election led likely-Republicans to increase their portfolio exposure to U.S. growth relative to likely-Democrats, and that this behavior holds even after controlling for various measures of differences in the real economic impact of the election, for example, only comparing people living in the same county and working for the same employer. These results are consistent with the pattern of survey beliefs reported in SectionII: households that report becoming more optimistic about the future growth of the U.S. economy increase their exposure to assets that load on future economic performance.34
The relative changes in portfolios that we find are not quantitatively large. This is due in part to the fact that we do not observe individual-level political affiliations, so that even heavily Republican or Democratic zip codes by our measures contain a mixture of Democratic and Republican investors, and in part to the fact that most of the wealth of RIs is retirement wealth, and there is very little (active) trading in retirement accounts. As prior research shows, retirement savers in the United States largely stick to their default portfolio allocations and trade very rarely. The next section considers these issues in greater detail.
The economic magnitudes of the coefficients on the other interacted explanatory variables also imply modest average effects of these other variables on portfolios. The coefficients on demographic variables such as age, wealth, income, gender, and marital status are statistically significant and likely pick up differences in economic impacts of the election outcome as well as differential changes in beliefs beyond what is captured by zip code affiliation. TableIIIshows that compared with these key investor characteristics, the coefficient on likely political affiliation is large. For example, in column (8) of TableIII, the magnitude of the postelection difference between likely-Republicans and likely-Democrats is equivalent to an age difference of 36 years, a wealth difference of 377 log points, and an income difference of 445 log points. Zip-code-level regressions in TableIA.XIII(see SectionIII.Cof theInternet Appendix) also show that apart from initial equity shares, zip code political affiliations are the most important variable for explaining postelection changes in portfolio allocations across zip codes. However, most of the variation in portfolio changes is due to idiosyncratic factors, especially at the individual level—the overallR2in TableIIIis 6.7% without fixed effects and 18.9% with employer-county fixed effects.
In the next section, we show that our estimated main effect is much larger among households who reallocate their wealth.
article.VI. Inaction and Large Portfolio Changes:
Having established our main results, we now show that the portfolio reallocation is due to a small share of active investors who make large reallocations in their portfolios.
First, we focus on the effect among active investors. TableIVperforms the same analyses as TableIIIon the subset of the population with an active portfolio reallocation during the year prior to the preelection year. An active portfolio reallocation is defined as an equity share change of at least 5% caused by trading in a particular month. As in our graphical analysis, we find much larger differential portfolio movements among active households. The relative increase in equity share among likely-Republicans who made active reallocations is three times as large as that for the general population of RIs.
We also analyze several other categorizations of more active or engaged investors, such as those invested less in target date funds and those with greater past access and trading behavior in their accounts. We report these results in TableIA.VIII. We find that the more active the investors included in the sample, the greater the differences in reallocation. Notably, relative reallocations were much greater—five times the baseline effect—among investors who made active reallocations in each of the three years prior to the election (1.7% of RIs). In contrast, investors who were less engaged, for example, those that have a significant part of their assets in target date funds or advised accounts, responded less than the average investor.35
We next turn to the size of the portfolio adjustments made by the investors who actively rebalance. As in our previous analysis, we continue to focus on hypothetical portfolios constructed as if there were no changes in valuations of funds or securities. This allows us to cleanly avoid changes in equity share driven by different returns on different investments. Panels A and B in Figure10decompose the changes in equity shares depicted in Figure8, Panel A, into small and large portfolio changes. In the upper panel, we take the average of (hypothetical) equity share changes relative to October 2016 interacted with an indicator for the change being at least 10%. In the lower panel, we multiply the (hypothetical) equity share change by an indicator for the change being at most 10%. We find no noticeable differential active adjustment among the investors who made only small adjustments, and hence, our measured effect comes from investors who made adjustments of greater than 10%. In terms of the share of investors who are making these large trades, in each month following the election, roughly 1% of investors made a trade of greater than 10%.
To provide further evidence on the extent to which our results are driven by large reallocations, we rerun our main analysis on the subsample of investors whose cumulative adjustment over the year following the election exceedsX%, for different values ofX. Strikingly, only about a quarter of the total relative portfolio reallocation came from investors who adjusted the equity shares of their portfolios by less than 25%. A full half of the measured effect comes from the few households who increased or decreased the equity share of their portfolio by 50% or more. FigureIA.13, Panel A, displays the complete set of results.
What share of investors were making these large changes? Aggregated across the year, 9.6% of our RI sample reallocated such that their equity share changed by at least 10%, 4.3% made a change of at least 25%, and 1.7% made a change of at least 50%.
We further find that rebalancing at the individual level is lumpy. Investors tend to make only one large trade. Examining the cumulative difference in reallocation between the most-Republican zip codes and the most-Democratic zip codes, we experiment with dropping all adjustments made by investors after their first rebalancing of 10% or greater. Almost the entire effect comes from the first large adjustment made by each investor (as shown in FigureIA.13, Panel B). In sum, effectively no Republican or Democratic investors actively changed their equity shares by only a few percentage points in response to the election. Instead, a small share of investors made very large changes in their portfolios in response to changes in their beliefs.
These results are consistent with previous studies that show that the typical American investor, whose wealth is primarily in retirement funds, trades very rarely (e.g., Choi et al. (2004)). The interesting question is why? The majority of wealth in our sample is retirement wealth, which has no fees associated with trading, and thus, large monetary costs are an implausible explanation. Instead, inaction seems to be due to psychological or informational costs. This interpretation is supported by our finding that investors who managed their portfolio allocations respond more strongly, while those who delegated or were less engaged with their allocations responded less. While it is possible that only a few households updated their beliefs following the election, this interpretation is inconsistent with the evidence from surveys of beliefs discussed in SectionII.
article.VII. Trading Volume:
A main motivation for and implication of models with time-varying heterogeneous beliefs is that they generate trade among agents, something that the canonical model is either silent about or, when calibrated, tends to vastly underpredict. In this section, we show that trading volume rose significantly following the election for our RI sample, consistent with these models and with our interpretation of the election as a public shock that led to differential changes in households' beliefs.
Figure11, Panel A, plots average trading volume over time and shows that the volume of trade by our sample of RIs rose significantly following the election. Their average trading volume was low (relative to turnover in the overall stock market), at roughly 2.25% per month, prior to the election, with active trades of roughly 1% per month. Trading increased significantly in the month following the election, reaching more than 3% per month in March 2017. Figure11, Panel B, shows that there was little difference in trading volume across the distribution of political affiliation in zip codes, including those that are most politically balanced. This result suggests that Republicans and Democrats in nonhomogeneous zip codes were both trading, just in opposite directions.
In contrast, there was no noticeable increase in trading activity in U.S. equity markets overall, even in the submarket for exchange-traded funds (ETFs). We confirm this with total U.S. equity market volume as well as with the narrower ETF market volume, both from the Chicago Board Options Exchange (we plot these series in FiguresIA.14, Panels A and B). In some sense, this is not surprising because our typical American retail investors trade very little. Thus, while the increase in trading that we document was a noticeable increase in their trading volume, it made only a trivial contribution to total trading volume. This observation also suggests that the political party-driven trading in and out of the stock market by institutional or high-net-worth individuals was also a trivial share of total trading volume.
We conclude that the evidence on trading volume supports the hypothesis that the news of the 2016 election was interpreted differently by people with different models of the world, but we also note that this particular source of belief variation can explain only a small amount of observed equity trades.
article.VIII. Differences across Households and:
Which investor characteristics are associated with the largest differential responses to the election news? We characterize differences in the effect of the election by running triple-difference regressions that capture differential effects by prior-year active rebalancing, age, wealth, and starting equity share. We report the coefficients on the Republican contribution share and its interactions in TableV, where age, log wealth, and lagged equity share are all demeaned.
The interaction of Republican contribution share and active reallocation in the prior year in column (2) confirms our previous results. The equity share response of households with active rebalancing in the prior year is almost four times as strong as that of other households. Turning to age in column (3), older households—those closer to retirement and that have more wealth and income—observe more portfolio differences by political affiliation than younger households. Relative to a base difference of 0.87%, a 10-year higher age is associated with an additional difference in equity share response of 0.51% between likely-Republicans and likely-Democrats. Column (4) shows that equity share also moves more for wealthier households, with a doubling of financial wealth implying a 0.25% greater increase in equity shares by likely-Republicans, and a doubling of income implying a 0.43% greater increase in equity shares. Further, the difference in reallocation is larger for investors with relatively low starting equity shares, as shown in column (5). With the full set of demographic interactions in column (6), age becomes less important, while heterogeneity in financial wealth, income, and initial equity allocation remain highly significant.
TableVIshows that differences in rebalancing across households are also related to the type of account they own. While investors with all types of accounts respond differently by party to the election, differences in responses are larger for people who have nonretirement accounts but more rebalancing occurs in retirement accounts. TableVI, column (1), presents our main results, which largely represent the response in retirement accounts since this is where the vast majority of our RIs hold their wealth. In column (2), we restrict the sample to households who own personally advised accounts.37Both pre- and postelection coefficients on political affiliation are somewhat larger, but with very large standard errors when the regression includes employer-county-year fixed effects. Column (3) shows a slightly lower but still highly significant estimate for the subset of households with a single, unmarried household head. Because we may only observe one member of a household, we are more confident that we observe the full portfolio of unmarried investors. A possible explanation for the lower magnitude in this sample is that these investors are typically younger with lower balances and a higher initial equity allocation.
In the remaining columns, we draw a distinction between retirement and nonretirement account behavior and owners. Given the prominence of retirement wealth in our sample, we find similar results when we restrict attention to retirement wealth (column (4)). We find that investors who own nonretirement accounts (column (5)) tended to trade more than people without such accounts (column (6)) and therefore show bigger responses, but for these investors, we find less reallocation in their nonretirement accounts (column (7)) than in their retirement accounts (column (8)), presumably due to both lower transaction costs and no immediate tax ramifications (see Dammon, Spatt, and Zhang (2001)). In sum, we find no evidence that reallocation happens more in nonretirement accounts that may offer more flexibility in choosing asset allocations or be associated with shorter investment horizons.
article.IX. Extensions:
In the political science literature, Gerber and Huber (2009,2010) study whether local economic activity is affected differently by elections in localities with different political affiliations. These papers and the subsequent literature focus primarily on consumption spending (McGrath (2017), Gillitzer and Prasad (2018), Benhabib and Spiegel (2019), Mian, Sufi, and Khoshkhou (2021)). While there is disagreement across studies, the evidence tends to suggest little to no differential effect of elections on local consumption levels across areas with different dominant political affiliations. This finding is striking because one would expect differential real effects of election outcomes on the current and future economic circumstances of people with different political affiliations. In contrast to this literature, we examine individual-level behavior controlling for the real effects on the local economy and labor income.
In this section, we investigate saving using three measures of account inflows. We find weak evidence that, relative to their Democratic counterparts, the typical Republican RI increased their savings in investment accounts by a small amount following the election.
A disadvantage of these first two measures of saving rates is that they are characterized by a lot of variation over time and across people because of large withdrawals and large inflows, presumably both due largely to transfers out of and into the financial institution rather than to actual saving. To address this issue, we measure the retirement saving rate as the contribution rate (as a percentage of income) chosen by the head of household in his or her active retirement account. The advantage of this measure is that it avoids account inflows and outflows that represent transfers from and to other institutions. However, this measure still does not avoid the possibility of substitution between retirement and nonretirement saving. Columns (7) and (8) of TableVIIshow that in the year prior to the election, the contribution rates of Democratic investors rose faster. Most of this difference can be explained by employer effects. There is no statistically significant difference in contribution rate changes postelection by party affiliation. The effects are also economically small, on the order of 0.05% of income.
We take this evidence as suggestive of only very small effects of the election on households' saving rates. If this were also true of consumption spending, this would have two implications. First, and less important given our extensive controls, a nonresponse of consumption would further mitigate the concern that differences in the economic effects of the policy changes cause the differences in portfolio responses that we find. Second, only certain utility functions are consistent with differences in beliefs that cause differences in portfolio exposures to risk without causing differences in consumption changes. Specifically, these findings are consistent with offsetting income and substitution effects.
The UMSC data also support this interpretation. As shown in Figure2, Panels A and B, in contrast to the large changes in beliefs about the future of the U.S. economy, there are only small changes in households' reported expectations about their own future income. In addition, the UMSC shows only small differential changes in responses to the questions: “Generally speaking, do you think now is a good or a bad time for people to buy major household items/a house?” (see FigureIA.15, Panels A and B). Consistent with a strong economy, most people believed that it was a good time to buy a house or major durable item before the election, with Democrats being slightly more optimistic than Republicans only for the purchase of a durable item. This difference disappeared after the election, with beliefs about the purchase of a durable item similar by party affiliation. For the purchase of a house, Republicans and Democrats held similar views prior to the election and Republicans became slightly more optimistic following the election.
The quantitative analysis so far concentrates on the equity share as a summary measure of investor portfolios. Figure8, Panel B, shows that the market beta of price-constant portfolios of Republicans also rose relative to Democrats after the election. Consistent with these raw effects, columns (1) to (3) of TableVIIIfind that likely-Republicans increased the market beta of their portfolios relative to likely-Democrats in the postelection year under the full set of controls. This subsection presents a more detailed decomposition of investor portfolios and shows that differential portfolio reallocation after the election concentrated on rebalancing across asset classes.
First, to what extent were differences in the portfolio market beta of people with different political affiliations due to differences in the market beta of the equity portfolio? Figure13, Panel A, shows both minimal differences in the market beta of equity by party affiliation and only small relative changes in the beta of equity following the election. Columns (4)–(6) of TableVIIIquantify these differences. Within equity, we do not see an increase in the market exposure of Republicans relative to Democrats, controlling for ex ante differences. This finding is consistent with our focus on the increase in the equity share of Republicans relative to Democrats in our main results.
Second, do we see differences in postelection reallocation across sectors? Stock market performance differs substantially across stocks and sectors expected to benefit from the unexpected change in party control (Wagner, Zeckhauser, and Ziegler (2018)). We have seen that there are differences in the ex ante composition of household equity holdings, but controlling for this heterogeneity does not alter our main results. In addition, we find no evidence of heterogeneous reallocation after the election. Columns (7) to (10) of TableVIIIshow insignificant coefficients for changes in the shares of equity in winning and losing sectors on the Republican contribution share. These results suggests that investors of different political affiliations do not disagree on the relative impact of the election on different sectors of the economy, and are inconsistent with differences in information or tastes as alternative explanations of our main finding that equity share changes differ by party affiliation. If Republicans were better able to identify firms or sectors that do well after the election or have more taste for holding those types of assets, we should observe significant differential changes in the composition of equity portfolios following the election. We do not observe such changes (see also the plots in FigureIA.16).
Third, do differences in beliefs about the future state of the U.S. economy translate into differential shifts into and out of international equity investments? We find no evidence of differential changes in international equity allocations.38Figure13, Panel B, shows the share of international equity across households sorted into quintiles by party affiliation of their zip code, relative to the end of October 2016. Prior to the election, there was a small relative decrease in the international share held by the most-Democratic zip codes and a similar small relative decrease following the election. Columns (11) and (12) of TableVIIIshow that in the regression with the full set of controls, there were no significant differential changes in the international equity share of Republican areas relative to Democratic areas.
The public signal of the election changed beliefs, leading to trade and portfolio reallocation across households. In this section, we ask whether the heterogeneous changes in beliefs changed the net equity demand of the RI sample that we observe, and whether the changes in beliefs and the resulting trade contributed to the high returns on the stock market following the election.
We can provide only rough answers to these questions. We begin by defining the baseline relative to which we measure the effect of differences in the updating of beliefs. We assume that there would have been no election-induced change in the demand for equity had the Democratic candidate won the election, that is, had the much more likely outcome occurred. Further, we base our calculation on our regressions using county vote share as the measure of political affiliation (see TableIA.V) and assume that there was no net change in equity demand in a county in which investable wealth is evenly split between Democrats and Republicans. Finally, we assume that wealth is uncorrelated with party affiliation within counties.
Under these three significant assumptions, the change in net equity demand from each county is our regression coefficient times county wealth times the difference between county vote share and 0.5. Summing across counties leads to a $660 million decrease in equity demand by the investors who we observe over the year following the election.
What about the aggregate demand for equity? We observe only a fraction of the RIs and wealth in each county. To scale our estimate to a measure for all RIs, we scale up the demand in each county to account for the share of the population that we observe in that county (based on U.S. Census data) and the national share of households who are RIs (from the 2016 SCF). Again, summing across counties, this crude estimate implies that equity demand declined by $1.09 billion among RIs in the United States. Finally, while we do not know much about the political affiliations of very wealthy households, if we simply scale this number up by the inverse of the share of the investable wealth in the United States held by RIs in the 2016 SCF, this back-of-the-envelope calculation implies that the election led to a decrease in net equity demand of $4.37 billion in the year following the election. This is obviously a very rough estimate, but far too small a number to have had a noticeable effect on returns during the year following the election.
Are our findings relevant for other elections, that is, would we expect Democrats and Republicans to trade differently following other major U.S. elections? We hypothesize that the answer is yes, although we expect these differences to be smaller for elections in which the candidates are more similar, the outcome is more likely ex ante, or (more speculatively) the nation is not as obsessed with the singular issue of the election (e.g., during times of less political polarization or of other major national news). In this section, we analyze reported beliefs around several U.S. elections, and provide one observation related to these hypotheses by running our analysis of changes in portfolio holding around the 2012 election.
We consider four U.S. presidential elections—2008, 2012, 2016, and 2020. Other than that in 2016, these elections contain no major upsets: in 2008, neither candidate was an incumbent president and the heavily favored candidate won, in 2012 the heavily favored incumbent president won, and in 2020 the nonincumbent favored candidate won (although uncertainty was not resolved immediately following the election). To analyze reported beliefs in each of these elections, we reply on UMSC data as in SectionIIbut now we use data from around each of these elections.39
According to the UMSC, the 2008 and 2012 elections were associated with smaller changes in reported beliefs by party than in 2016, consistent with less movement in reported beliefs when the more likely election outcome occurs.
